Section 1 (Chapter 3): Purkait, N. (2019), Neural Networks with Keras, Packt.  [https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038)   

From <[https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-4-machine-learning-using-cloud-computing-ai-1698/?context_id=15077&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-4-machine-learning-using-cloud-computing-ai-1698/?context_id=15077&context_resource=lp)>

# Books

## Section 1 (Chapter 3): Purkait, N. (2019), Neural Networks with Keras, Packt.  [https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038)   

Traditional ML might be around training a model what cancer is. Deep learning is when you show it lots of pictures and let it figure out what's important.

Can use simple representations, which build up to more complex representations in subsequent layers

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Avoid memorization, can do via dropout regularization and weighted parameter.

A tensors has

- Rank - number of axis, e.g. rank 2 is a matrix, a 2-dimensional tensor. Indicated as ndim
- Shape - Number of dimensions on each axis
- Content - Type of data, float32, unit8, float64

Three-dimensional tensor is like a cub of numbers

Four-dimensional tensor could be an array of 3d tensors

Shape (400, 600, 3) could be used to represent a 400x600 image

NumPy has arrays

Pandas has dataframes

Keras has models

Can use kernal_initializer and bias_initializer to set initial states. Initialization can significantly boost learning.

Kernal_initilizer

- Glorot_uniform: Weights from a sample of a uniform distribution between -limit and limit. Limit is sqrt(6/(fan_in + fan_out)). Fan_in is the number of input units in the weight tensor and fan_out is the number of units of outputs units in the weight tensor
- Random_uniform: uniform from -0.05 and 0.05
- Random_normal. From a gaussian distribution ranging from -0.05 and 0.05
- Zero: weights are initially zero

## Keras activations

Recommended to experiment with different ones.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Could have softmax on output layer to convert to a probability distriubtion

### Compile has three args

- A loss function. Measures the performance on the training data compared to true output labels. e.g. Mean Squared Error a(MSE).

- If you are doing binary classification, there is binary cross-entropy.
- For more than two categories, there is categorical cross-entropy or sparse categorical cross-entropy.
- Sequence data there is Connectionist Temporal Classification(CTC)
- Cosine_proximity
- Count data maybe Poisson loss function.

- An optimizer. How to get to a global minimum loss. Used to update weight and biases.

- Constant learning rates like Stochastic Gradient Decent(SGD)
- Adaptive learning rates like Adagrad, Adadelta, RMSprop and Adam

- Metrics. The evaluation benchmarks we monitor during training and testing. Accuracy is commonly used but can implement custom metrics. Not used directly in training.

### Fitting the model.

Can have arguments to shuffle data, create validation splits, give custom weights to output classes.

Shuffle and help prevent overfit.

Custom weights can be useful if some classes are underrepresented. Pass class_weights with a dictionary that maps indices to custom weights.

Epochs. Number of times to go through the entire dataset. Too low and it will be too simplistic and too high will result in overfitting.

Batch_size. Number of samples that will propagate through the network per training iteration. How many it sees before updating model weights. Trains faster in mini batches vs after each example. Stochastic is a batch size of one. Mini-batch is a smaller batch.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Number of iterations. The number of passes, for one epoch number of examples/batch size

If the accuracy during the training dataset is higher than test that might be an example of overfitting.

## Regularization

Regularization is a technique used to enhance the generalizability of a model and mitigate the risk of overfitting.

How to prevent model from learning misleading or irrelevant patterns. Could be more data.

If the model could only memorise a small number of patters, it's forced to be efficient and learn more generalised patterns.

If the sizer of the network is too big it can make it worse at categorising generalised data

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Regularizing the weights

To prevent picking up irrelevant features.

Limit complexity by limiting its layer weights to only take small values. Do it by adding a cost to the loss function. Penalising neurons for having larger weights.

- L1 regularization. Add a cost proportional to the absolute value of the weighted coefficients
- L2 regularization. Add a cost that is proportional to the square of the value of the weighted coefficients.
- Elastic net regularization. Uses a combination of L1 and L2

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Using dropout layers

A low dropout rate might not provide the robustness we want, but a high dropout rate might mean the model can't learn anything. Normally use a value between 0.2 and 0.4

Can see when overfitting is resolved

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Increasing complexity can have a quasi-exponential effect on time

## Language processing

Vectorise the data.

### One-hot encoding

If there are 12k words, the longest review can only be 12k in length. Can make a binary vector of 12k length

So a review of "bad movie", would just have two item in the 12k as 1 and the rest as zeroes.

One-hot encoding is used to encode features and categorical labels alike.

Can pass validation dataset to fit, so you can see how it does over data that's not updating the model.

Can assign model.fit to an object, which is a history object, it's recorded by a callback

One hot is used for categorical variables. Maybe one-hot isn't great for word embeddings.

Maybe it's better to just have a list like [2, 32, 43243]

### Callbacks

If you want to reset model before each fit, you can use a callback.

early_stopping= keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(monitor='loss')

network_metadata=model.fit(x_train, y_train, validation_data=(x_test,  y_test), epochs=20, batch_size=100,                             callbacks=[early_stopping])

Good metrics to monitor are validation loss or validation accuracy. Don't use training accuracy or loss.

Here you can see while training acc increases validation acc decreases. So actually it's best at 1 epoch and more makes it worse.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Model prediction, where predicting from say a list of 10 then softmax is good. If choosing binary then signoid is good.

### Feature-wise normalization

If there are lots of features, it's best to scale them my subtracting the mean and dividing by the SD.

Can't do this for all since might not have that data so there are other methods like log normalization

For regression modelling, we remove activation function in the last layer and just use one output neuron.

Would want to use MSE loss function and monitor training progress using Mean Absolute Error(MAE)

MSE emphasises the spread by penalising errors that are further away. But MAE is fine to measure the metric.

### k-fold cross validation

If there isn't enough data, there might not be enough for validation.

Split data in say five splits, then has give models, trains on four of the splits and tests on final one. Then average the predictions across the five models.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

# Main

## ML Pipeline

1. Data sourcing
2. Data Preparation
3. Training
4. Validation/optimisation
5. Testing
6. Deployment
7. Review

![Illustration of an example machine learning pipeline as described in the text.](data:image/png;base64,)

Data sourcing

APIs, SQL, bulk download uploading

Data preparation

Usually takes the longest.

Decides which elements to use, dice/slice/link them to make analytics data set.

Need to clear the data, sort out anomalies, handle missing values and scale the data fields, one-hot encoding.

Training

Data set following preparation needs to be split into training and testing sets.

Validation/optimisation

Training, validation and final testing set.

Training is used to train.

Validation set is used to tun the model's hyperparameters and make decisions about model selection

Test set is used to provide an unbiased evaluation of the final model's performance.

Testing

Test on unseen data.

Deployment

Develop suitable interfaces for the intended users and training the users.

Might be done by a delivery team, but data scientists are likely to be needed for consultation.

Review

From time to time the model needs to be reviewed. The world changes so the original models might not be suitable.

## Deep learning frameworks

TensorFlow

Pytorch

Keras

CNTK

CAffe2

MXNet

Gluon

Chainer

[https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/deep-learning-frameworks-compared-mxnet-vs-tensorflow-vs-dl4j-vs-pytorch/#:~:text=Compared%20to%20TensorFlow%2C%20MXNet%20has,not%20as%20popular%20as%20Tensorflow](https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/deep-learning-frameworks-compared-mxnet-vs-tensorflow-vs-dl4j-vs-pytorch/#:~:text=Compared%20to%20TensorFlow%2C%20MXNet%20has,not%20as%20popular%20as%20Tensorflow)

Tensorflow is a commonly used framework built by Google and is open-source.

Advantages

- User friendly. Easy to learn
- Tensorboard for monitoring and visualising.
- Community support
- Can use TensorFlow Lite to run on mobile devices
- Tensorflow.js let's you run real-time deep learning models in browsers

Disadvantages

- Can be slower than MxNet and CNTK
- Debugging can be challenging
- No support for OpenCL

Apache MXNet supports a variety of language like JS, python, c++.

Advantages

- Efficient, scalable and fast
- Supported by all major platforms
- Provides multi-GPU support
- Supports scala, R, python, c++ and js.
- Easy model serving and high performance API

Disadvantages

- Smaller community than TensorFlow's
- Changes take longer to be implemented

Microsoft CNTK is open-source. Written in C++ but supports C#, python, c++, java.

Advantages

- Reliable and good performance
- Scalability makes it good for enterprises
- Numerous optimized components
- Easy to integrate with Apache Spark
- Works well with Azure Cloud
- Resource usage and management are efficient

Disadvantages

- Minimal community support compared to TensorFlow, but has dedicated of microsoft engineers working full time on it
- Learning curve.

PyTorch is developed by Facebook.

Advantages

- User-friendly design and structure that makes constructing deep learning models transparent
- Has useful debugging tools like PyCharm debugger
- Contains many pre-trained models and supports distributed training
- Image recognition, NLP and reinforcement learning are areas where it shines.

Disadvantages

- Does not have interfaces for monitoring and visualization like TensorFlow
- It's a newer framework with less community support.

DeepLearning4j, good framework for Java and Scala.

Advantages

- Scalable and easily processes large amounts of data
- Easy integration with Apache Spark
- Excellent community support and documentation.

Disadvantages

- Limited to Java/Scala
- Less popular than Tensorflow and PyTorch.

![Deep learning frameworks 202417: Amazon AWS – MXnet and Gluon, Microsoft – CNTK, Torch – PyTorch, Facebook and Caffe – Caffe2, Theano – Keras, and Google – TensorFlow.](data:image/png;base64,)

## TensorFlow and Keras

TensorFlow Works with GPU, CPU and TPU on desktops, server clusters, mobile and edge devices

TensorFlow 1 is widely used but is lower level than many developers appreciate.

TensorFlow 2 was developed to alleviate this and uses Keras as a front end API.

Keras is a user-fiendly API on top of TensorFlow that offers consistent and simple APIs minimising the number of actions required for common use cases and provides actionable error messages.

- Easy and fast prototyping
- Support for implementation of several of the latest neural network architectures and pretaied models and exercise data sets
- Executes impeccably on CPU and GPU

![The speedy way in which ideas for machine learning solutions can be developed – idea (deep learning framework – Keras), experiment (infrastructure) and results (visualisation framework: TensorBoard).](data:image/png;base64,)

- Keras (n.d.) Keras Simple Powerful Flexible, Website, Available at: Keras: the Python deep learning API (Accessed 05/07/2022). Available at:  [https://keras.io/](https://keras.io/)
